segs.utf8: [Music] [Applause]
[Music]

I'm sorry your dad is sick, but who's going to do 
this work? 13 words. That's all it took to create

a storm in my life that would change everything. 
And just like a sailboat caught in a current,

I got thrown off course by my manager's 
words because I was unanchored. You see,

when we become unanchored at work, we 
typically have reactions that make us

culprits in helping enable bad leadership. 
And I've been guilty of it. You see, I was

in a company that I loved. I spent 10 years 
there and I was considered a high performer.

I had promotions and accolades to prove it. But 
after one year with this leader, I was crashing

out fast, chasing a line of success that just kept 
moving. And my colleagues and I, we trauma bonded,

trying to survive a leader whose cruelty was 
as predictable as the tide, suffocating us with

micromanagement and then vanishing when we needed 
support. But that day, that day was different.

You see, my dad had been sent to the emergency 
room. My mom called me and told me I needed to

get home to Jersey 3 hours away. Our team was lean 
so that I knew my absence would be inconvenient as

emergencies usually are. So, I created a plan and 
I shared it with my manager and my circumstance.

And her response, I'm sorry your dad is sick, but 
who's going to do this work? And just like that,

I was caught in the perfect storm. A family crisis 
and a career under siege. I didn't know what to

do. I'd come later to realize that I was facing 
a tsunami of epic proportions. And so many of

people have faced it themselves. And it capsizes 
careers worldwide. I had a boss that sucked.

[Applause] Yeah, sucked. I said it 
because we've all had something suck

the life out of us. Mosquitoes, 
ticks, fleas, mother-in-laws.

My mother-in-law is actually amazing. 
N bad bosses. You know you have a bad

boss because when you see the email with 
their name on it or you hear their voice,

your body reacts. Your stomach gets tied into 
knots and your your palms get sweaty and you

might even get a stress headache and the only 
thing you can think is, "Oh, dear God, what now?"

I've heard it said that when we're 
unanchored, this is what happens.

After two and a half decades of leading 
HR and corporate talent management,

I now consult CEOs, executives, and 
HR leaders and entrepreneurs on how

to create good cultures. And I found three 
things to be true. We're all in one at least

one of these groups. You either have or had a 
boss that sucks. You are the boss that sucks.

or you're one of the few unicorns that's never had 
a bad boss, but you do know the damage it can do.

And those 13 words damaged and disoriented me. 
And that's what's happening to people worldwide.

When we're unanchored at work, we get damaged and 
disoriented. And the surveys prove it. You see,

according to Gallup, seven out of 10 of us 
in this room are some kind of disengaged. And

50% of people, they leave companies because of 
the managers, not the company. And when Forbes

asked managers who needs leadership training, 
nearly 100% of the leaders said that other

leaders needed leadership training. Kind of 
like a bad boyfriend. Girl, it's you, not me.

Now, while we're staggered by the numbers, what 
we're not usually staggers me is our response to

it. Let somebody talk to you about having a 
bad boss and you say, "Okay, so what?" We've

all had a bad boss. We've been there. Our silent 
acceptant of bad leadership is a problem. We've

subconsciously accepted it and become so good 
at managing it, we haven't even stopped to think

that we are the ones keeping it alive. And I think 
it's time we change that. According to his book,

and this really is the title, Jerry Harvey wrote 
a book called How Come Every Time I Get Stabbed

in the Back, My Fingerprints Are on the Knife? He 
gears up the idea that our greatest career wounds

often have our fingerprints on the weapons. 
Have you ever been stabbed emotionally at

work? Perhaps stabbed by the boss who asks you 
for your opinion, but doesn't really want it.

Yes. Or by the boss who consistently and 
incessantly procrastinates that causes you

more work at 459. Or what about a leader whose 
toxic positivity creates a culture that makes us

all feel like we have to walk around like this? 
Our culture is a culture of mandatory happiness.

When bad bosses happen to us, we typically take to 
our favorite modes of communication to complain,

to commiserate, or condemn. And that's where our 
fingerprints get put on the weapons. Every time

we're silent to our own or someone else's 
suffering, we give a subtle fingerprint of

permission to bad bossy. I think it's time we 
actively reject the notion that bad leadership

is something that we have to endure like 
the weather. And I think it's time we drop

anger. I want to share with you a framework. 
It's called anchor. It's an acronym. Assess,

negotiate, consult, heed, observe, and resolve. 
I believe this framework is going to help us

all move from accomplice to advocate and from 
complicit to courageous. First, I want you to

assess the situation without your emotions because 
they typically hijack the way you see life. Next,

I want you to negotiate your goals, long-term and 
near-term, personal and professional. The journey

of a thousand miles starts with one step. Plan the 
step, but remember, the goal is the journey. Next,

I want you to heed your triggers. Do 
you know what pushes your buttons?

Author Gabby Bernstein says that we need to 
know our triggers and plan ways around them

and routines to help us not get triggered. Also 
observe your leaders patterns. Managers are and

people are oddly predictable. When we know the 
patterns of people, we can plan better. Figure

out the pattern so that you can plan around 
your leaders issues or at least try to lessen

the impact. And finally, resolve. Resolve that 
their definition of success may be unachievable

or unattainable, but yours doesn't have to 
be. I didn't have an anchor that day. So,

I did what most of us would do. I grabbed 
a life preserver and I saved myself.

I didn't face the conflict. Instead, I went 
into silent compliance and completed a 13-hour

day based on all the new tasks that were given to 
me. And the last thing I did was to fill out leave

paperwork so that I could be home with my family 
and protect my job. And it's a decision that I

don't regret because it allowed me to be with my 
dad when he took his last breath two months later.

But what if I had an anchor instead of a life 
preserver? Perhaps I would have assessed that

her priorities were her priorities and my family 
emergency was my priority and I would not have

been mad that they happened at the same time. 
Perhaps I would have negotiated to understand

what her immediate needs were, but I would have 
invited her to help me solve the problem of how to

get home more quickly. I would have counseledled 
with leaders who invited me to figure out how to

have the conflict in a productive way rather 
than settle for silent compliance. Perhaps

then I would have heeded my trigger. I wanted 
approval from my leader and I wanted to have

a good relationship. But if I had observed her 
patterns, I would have realized what I had been

ignoring for months. Our relationship was as 
transactional as an ATM machine. And I would

have resolved not to allow myself to pick between 
my job and my family. When we're caught in the

current of powerful, terrible leadership, dropping 
an anchor is exactly what we need. And my anchor,

it would have sounded like this. I understand 
the work that you have for me is important to

you and my family is important to me. So, what 
work can I do for you in the next hour before

I leave? And what work do I need to do while 
I'm in Jersey at the bedside of my dying dad?

The anchor stops the drift. The anchor creates 
stability. And the anchor allows you to not get

sucked into the current of someone else's 
challenges. It doesn't stop the flow or the

current. It just allows the current to flow 
around you. Keep doing what angers do best,

keeping you grounded in your 
values and your priorities. Now,

let me be clear. All managers 
are not bad. Most of them are.

But globally, the room for improvement is 
somewhere between here and Mars. So, here's

what I want to encourage you. I want to encourage 
each and every one of us to take accountability.

Move from accomplice to advocate. Move from 
complicit to courageous and drop your anchor.

Because when we do that, we create new waves 
of accountability from our leadership. Facing

the current is hard and sometimes it hurts. Being 
hurt is a problem, but staying hurt is a choice.

So here's what I'm asking. The next time 
you get into a bad leadership situation,

don't phone a friend. Don't call your mama. 
Don't commiserate. Drop your anchor. Say the

quiet part out loud. Advocate for 
your needs. Because when we do,

we create accountability. And perhaps, 
just perhaps, dropping our anchors and

holding them steady is exactly what we need to 
change the tide of bad leadership. [Applause]
